data.label: EN-01
data.claim: Climate change is a hoax invented by scientists to get research funding.
data.name: scenario:EN-01

data.label: ExxonMobil / Fossil Fuel Industry
data.motivation: ExxonMobil's own scientists confirmed the CO2-warming link in 1977-1982. Internal memos documented the science accurately. Despite this, the company funded doubt campaigns from the late 1980s through the 2010s. The motivation was protecting the value of fossil fuel reserves and delaying regulation. Supran & Oreskes (Science, 2023) demonstrated that Exxon's internal projections from the late 1970s closely matched observed warming. The company's public communications systematically contradicted its own internal science.
data.name: actor:exxonmobil-fossil-fuel-industry

data.label: American Petroleum Institute (API)
data.motivation: Trade association representing 600+ oil and gas companies. Produced the 1998 'Global Climate Science Communications Plan' with explicit strategy to manufacture uncertainty. Budget of $5M+ for the doubt campaign. The memo stated victory would be achieved when the public perceived uncertainty in climate science.
data.name: actor:american-petroleum-institute-api

data.label: Heartland Institute
data.motivation: Libertarian think tank that organized International Conferences on Climate Change (2008-present) and produced NIPCC reports. Previously defended the tobacco industry. Funded by fossil fuel interests including anonymous donors exposed in leaked 2012 documents. The Heartland strategy documents (leaked 2012) discussed plans to develop K-12 curriculum materials questioning climate science.
data.name: actor:heartland-institute

data.label: Senator James Inhofe (R-OK)
data.motivation: As Chair of the Senate Environment and Public Works Committee (2003-2007, 2015-2017), Inhofe used his platform to promote climate denial. Published 'The Greatest Hoax: How the Global Warming Conspiracy Threatens Your Future' (2012). Oklahoma is the fifth-largest oil-producing state. Inhofe received more fossil fuel industry campaign contributions than almost any other senator.
data.name: actor:senator-james-inhofe-r-ok

data.label: Koch Brothers / Koch Industries / Americans for Prosperity
data.motivation: Charles and David Koch (David died 2019) built Koch Industries into the largest privately held energy company in the US. They funded a network of organizations (Americans for Prosperity, Cato Institute, Heritage Foundation) that promoted climate denial. Greenpeace's 'Koch Industries: Secretly Funding the Climate Denial Machine' (2010) documented over $100 million in funding to climate denial groups. Americans for Prosperity pressured Republican lawmakers to oppose climate legislation.
data.name: actor:koch-brothers-koch-industries-americans-for-prosperity

data.label: Richard Lindzen (MIT)
data.motivation: MIT atmospheric physicist and the most credentialed contrarian voice. Argued that climate sensitivity is low and that feedback effects will dampen warming. His 'iris hypothesis' (2001) proposed that tropical cloud changes would counteract warming; subsequent research has not supported it. Acknowledged receiving $2,500/day consulting fees from fossil fuel companies.
data.name: actor:richard-lindzen-mit

data.label: Fox News / Conservative Media Ecosystem
data.motivation: Climate denial content generates high audience engagement. Fox News has consistently framed climate science as politically motivated, featuring contrarian voices disproportionately relative to their representation in the scientific community. The network's coverage shifted from 'debating the science' to 'attacking the scientists' after ClimateGate.
data.name: actor:fox-news-conservative-media-ecosystem

data.label: NASA GISS / NOAA / Hadley Centre / Berkeley Earth
data.motivation: Independent scientific organizations maintaining temperature records using independent methodologies. NASA GISS (Goddard Institute for Space Studies), NOAA NCEI (National Centers for Environmental Information), Hadley Centre (UK Met Office), and Berkeley Earth (originally funded by Koch-affiliated foundation) all produce consistent warming records.
data.name: actor:nasa-giss-noaa-hadley-centre-berkeley-earth

data.label: IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change)
data.motivation: UN body that assesses the state of climate science through comprehensive literature reviews. Involves thousands of scientists in authoring and reviewing roles. The IPCC does not conduct original research; it assesses published research. The government approval process for the Summary for Policymakers typically results in more conservative (weaker) language, not alarmist language.
data.name: actor:ipcc-intergovernmental-panel-on-climate-change

data.label: US Department of Defense
data.motivation: The DoD treats climate change as a national security threat based on its own intelligence assessments. The Quadrennial Defense Reviews (2010, 2014), the DoD Climate Adaptation Plan (2014, 2021), and CNA Military Advisory Board reports identify climate change as a 'threat multiplier' for geopolitical instability. Military planning operates on a decades-long time horizon and has no institutional incentive to fabricate climate threats.
data.name: actor:us-department-of-defense

data.label: Global Insurance and Reinsurance Industry
data.motivation: Munich Re, Swiss Re, and Lloyd's of London incorporate climate risk into actuarial models based on observed loss data. Their motivation is profit: accurate risk assessment is the core of the insurance business model. If climate change were a hoax, their loss models would be wrong and they would be overcharging customers (losing them to competitors who priced risk correctly).
data.name: actor:global-insurance-and-reinsurance-industry

data.label: Social Media Platforms and Algorithms
data.motivation: Engagement-maximizing algorithms amplify climate denial content because it generates outrage, debate, and sharing. Platform design favors emotionally charged, simple narratives ('it's a hoax!') over nuanced scientific explanation. Internal Facebook research (Haugen disclosures, 2021) documented preferential amplification of divisive content.
data.name: actor:social-media-platforms-and-algorithms

data.name: era:era-1-scientific-discovery-and-fossil-fuel-awareness

data.name: era:era-3-post-climategate-and-social-media-amplification

data.label: EN-02
data.claim: GMOs are poisoning our food supply and Monsanto knows it.
data.name: scenario:EN-02

data.label: Monsanto (acquired by Bayer, 2018)
data.motivation: Commercial revenue from glyphosate herbicides and patented seed traits. Monsanto's Roundup brand generated approximately $4.8 billion annual revenue at peak. The company's business model depended on farmers purchasing both Roundup Ready seeds and Roundup herbicide. Documented history of aggressive patent enforcement, regulatory influence, and ghostwriting of scientific papers to protect glyphosate's market position.
data.name: actor:monsanto-acquired-by-bayer-2018

data.label: Organic food industry (trade organizations and major brands)
data.motivation: Commercial revenue from organic product premium pricing. The US organic food market reached $67.6 billion in 2023 (Organic Trade Association). Organic certification by USDA definition prohibits GMO ingredients, making anti-GMO sentiment a direct commercial driver. Key trade organizations include the Organic Consumers Association (OCA) and the Non-GMO Project (whose 'Butterfly' label appears on over 60,000 products generating licensing revenue).
data.name: actor:organic-food-industry-trade-organizations-and-major-brands

data.label: Jeffrey Smith / Institute for Responsible Technology
data.motivation: Author and activist career built on anti-GMO position. Published 'Seeds of Deception' (2003) and produced 'Genetic Roulette' documentary (2012). Revenue from book sales, speaking fees, and organizational donations. Smith has no formal training in genetics, biology, or food science.
data.name: actor:jeffrey-smith-institute-for-responsible-technology

data.label: Gilles-Eric Seralini / CRIIGEN
data.motivation: Academic career and activist positioning. Seralini is a molecular biologist at the University of Caen, France. His 2012 study claiming GM corn caused tumors in rats was retracted by Food and Chemical Toxicology (2013) due to insufficient sample size and inconclusive results, then republished in Environmental Sciences Europe (2014) without additional peer review of the original criticisms.
data.name: actor:gilles-eric-seralini-criigen

data.label: Vandana Shiva
data.motivation: Environmental and anti-globalization activism. Shiva positions GMOs within a broader critique of corporate agricultural monopolies, seed sovereignty, and neocolonialism. Revenue from speaking fees (reported at $40,000+ per appearance), book sales, and Navdanya foundation donations.
data.name: actor:vandana-shiva

data.label: US Right to Know (USRTK)
data.motivation: Investigative nonprofit focused on corporate influence in food policy. Founded by Gary Ruskin (2014). Funded in part by the Organic Consumers Association. Conducts FOIA campaigns targeting public university scientists who have received industry funding.
data.name: actor:us-right-to-know-usrtk

data.label: Major scientific bodies (NAS, EFSA, WHO, Royal Society)
data.motivation: Scientific assessment and public health protection. The National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine published a comprehensive 388-page review in 2016 ('Genetically Engineered Crops: Experiences and Prospects').
data.name: actor:major-scientific-bodies-nas-efsa-who-royal-society

data.label: International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC)
data.motivation: Hazard identification for cancer risk classification. IARC is a specialized agency of the WHO. Its monograph program classifies substances by whether they CAN cause cancer (hazard) rather than whether they DO cause cancer at typical exposure levels (risk).
data.name: actor:international-agency-for-research-on-cancer-iarc

data.label: Anti-GMO ballot initiative campaigns
data.motivation: GMO labeling mandates that would increase costs for conventional products and strengthen market position of organic alternatives. Major campaigns: California Prop 37 (2012, defeated), Washington I-522 (2013, defeated), Oregon Measure 92 (2014, narrowly defeated), Vermont Act 120 (2014, passed, preempted by federal law in 2016).
data.name: actor:anti-gmo-ballot-initiative-campaigns

data.label: Joseph Mercola / Mercola.com
data.motivation: Commercial revenue from alternative health products. Mercola.com is one of the most visited alternative health websites globally. Mercola sells supplements, organic food products, and health devices. Anti-GMO content drives traffic that converts to product sales.
data.name: actor:joseph-mercola-mercola-com

data.label: Russian state media (RT, Sputnik)
data.motivation: Strategic amplification of anti-GMO narratives aligns with Russia's domestic GMO ban (2016) and broader strategy of undermining trust in Western regulatory institutions. Russia banned GMO cultivation in 2016 (Federal Law No. 358-FZ) while still importing GMO animal feed.
data.name: actor:russian-state-media-rt-sputnik

data.label: Carey Gillam / The Guardian US
data.motivation: Investigative journalist and author. Former Reuters reporter who covered agrochemicals. Published 'Whitewash: The Story of a Weed Killer, Cancer, and the Corruption of Science' (2017). Now research director at USRTK.
data.name: actor:carey-gillam-the-guardian-us

data.label: EV-02
data.claim: The 2020 US presidential election was stolen through widespread voter fraud.
data.name: scenario:EV-02

data.label: Donald Trump / Trump Campaign
data.motivation: Retention of political power and avoidance of electoral defeat. Trump's refusal to concede was personally and politically motivated: acceptance of defeat threatened his brand identity as a 'winner,' exposed him to potential criminal liability (as later materialized in multiple indictments), and jeopardized his ability to run again. The 'stolen election' narrative preserved his political viability and maintained his base's loyalty.
data.name: actor:donald-trump-trump-campaign

data.label: Rudy Giuliani
data.motivation: Personal loyalty to Trump, desire to remain relevant in Republican politics, and financial incentive (legal fees). Giuliani served as Trump's personal attorney and was the public face of the legal challenges.
data.name: actor:rudy-giuliani

data.label: Sidney Powell
data.motivation: Personal brand building and ideological conviction. Powell positioned herself as an anti-establishment crusader. She promoted the most extreme claims (Dominion-Venezuela connection, Hugo Chavez involvement despite his death in 2013).
data.name: actor:sidney-powell

data.label: Mike Lindell (MyPillow CEO)
data.motivation: Personal conviction combined with commercial interest. Lindell spent tens of millions of his own money promoting stolen election claims. His MyPillow brand became tied to the MAGA movement, generating both loyal customers and significant losses from retail deplatforming.
data.name: actor:mike-lindell-mypillow-ceo

data.label: Fox News / Fox Corporation
data.motivation: Audience retention and advertising revenue. Internal communications revealed during the Dominion v. Fox News lawsuit showed Fox hosts and executives privately acknowledged the fraud claims were false but amplified them to avoid losing viewers to Newsmax and OAN.
data.name: actor:fox-news-fox-corporation

data.label: Newsmax / One America News Network (OAN)
data.motivation: Market opportunity to capture Fox News viewers by being more aligned with Trump's stolen election narrative. Both outlets provided more aggressive coverage of fraud claims than Fox initially did, creating competitive pressure on Fox to amplify similar content.
data.name: actor:newsmax-one-america-news-network-oan

data.label: True the Vote / Catherine Engelbrecht and Gregg Phillips
data.motivation: Organizational fundraising, ideological commitment to voter fraud claims that predates 2020. True the Vote has promoted voter fraud allegations since 2010. The 2020 election provided their largest platform and fundraising opportunity.
data.name: actor:true-the-vote-catherine-engelbrecht-and-gregg-phillips

data.label: Dinesh D'Souza
data.motivation: Content monetization and ideological commitment. D'Souza produced '2000 Mules' (2022), which became the most commercially successful piece of stolen election content. Previously convicted of campaign finance fraud (2014), pardoned by Trump (2018).
data.name: actor:dinesh-d-souza

data.label: State-level Republican officials who certified results
data.motivation: Adherence to legal duty and institutional integrity. Multiple Republican officials in swing states certified Biden's win despite intense pressure from Trump and allies.
data.name: actor:state-level-republican-officials-who-certified-results

data.label: CISA (Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency)
data.motivation: Institutional mandate to secure election infrastructure. CISA, under Trump-appointed director Christopher Krebs, issued a statement calling the 2020 election 'the most secure in American history.'
data.name: actor:cisa-cybersecurity-and-infrastructure-security-agency

data.label: Department of Justice / AG William Barr
data.motivation: Barr was a strong Trump ally who expanded executive power arguments but found no evidence of widespread fraud. His December 1, 2020 statement to the AP contradicting Trump's fraud claims came from the highest law enforcement authority in the government.
data.name: actor:department-of-justice-ag-william-barr

data.label: Federal and state courts (60+ judges)
data.motivation: Judicial mandate to evaluate evidence under legal standards. The 60+ judges who heard election cases included Trump appointees and judges across the political spectrum.
data.name: actor:federal-and-state-courts-60-judges

data.label: QAnon and adjacent online communities
data.motivation: The stolen election narrative integrated seamlessly into the existing QAnon framework ('the storm,' 'the great awakening'). QAnon influencers, including Ron Watkins (8kun administrator who posed as an election security expert), amplified Dominion and fraud claims to their large audiences.
data.name: actor:qanon-and-adjacent-online-communities

data.label: Save America PAC / Trump fundraising apparatus
data.motivation: Direct financial profit from the stolen election narrative. Trump's fundraising emails and texts citing election fraud raised over $250 million from November 2020 through January 2021 through the Save America PAC and related entities.
data.name: actor:save-america-pac-trump-fundraising-apparatus

data.label: EV-03
data.claim: The 2020 US presidential election was stolen through widespread voter fraud.
data.name: scenario:EV-03

data.label: Fox News
data.motivation: Fox News amplified fraud claims, particularly through hosts Maria Bartiromo, Lou Dobbs, and Jeanine Pirro. The Dominion Voting Systems lawsuit discovery revealed that Fox hosts and executives (including Tucker Carlson, Sean Hannity, Laura Ingraham, and Rupert Murdoch) privately acknowledged the claims were false while continuing to broadcast them. Internal messages showed the network's motivation was fear of losing audience to Newsmax and OAN, which were promoting even more extreme fraud claims. Rupert Murdoch acknowledged in his deposition that he could have intervened but did not.
data.name: actor:fox-news

data.label: Newsmax and OAN
data.motivation: Both networks promoted fraud claims more aggressively than Fox News, attempting to capture Fox's audience from the right. Newsmax's viewership surged in November-December 2020 as it positioned itself as the network that would 'tell the truth' about election fraud. OAN was even more extreme. Both face ongoing defamation litigation from Dominion and Smartmatic. Newsmax settled with Smartmatic during trial (September 2025).
data.name: actor:newsmax-and-oan

data.label: Brad Raffensperger (Georgia Secretary of State, Republican)
data.motivation: Georgia's Republican Secretary of State who oversaw the election and certified Biden's win after three recounts. He received death threats from Trump supporters. He published the recorded phone call in which Trump asked him to 'find 11,780 votes.' He represents the Republican officials who chose election integrity over party loyalty at significant personal cost.
data.name: actor:brad-raffensperger-georgia-secretary-of-state-republican

data.label: AG William Barr
data.motivation: Trump's Attorney General who investigated fraud claims and found 'no evidence of fraud on a scale that could have affected a different outcome in the election' (December 1, 2020, AP interview). Barr was generally regarded as one of Trump's most loyal cabinet members, making his refusal to endorse the fraud narrative particularly significant. He resigned on December 23, 2020.
data.name: actor:ag-william-barr

data.label: Dominion Voting Systems
data.motivation: Canadian-American voting technology company falsely accused of rigging the election. Their defamation lawsuits have been the most effective mechanism for establishing the falsity of the fraud claims. The Fox News settlement ($787.5M) and the Giuliani judgment ($148M) demonstrated that courts found the claims against Dominion to be false and defamatory.
data.name: actor:dominion-voting-systems

data.label: January 6 Committee
data.motivation: House Select Committee that investigated the January 6 Capitol breach and the broader effort to overturn the 2020 election. Conducted 1,000+ witness interviews, collected 125,000+ documents, and held 10 public hearings. Key findings: Trump was told by advisors that he had lost, he pressed forward anyway, and the 'Stop the Steal' operation raised $250M+ with misleading claims about how the money would be used.
data.name: actor:january-6-committee

data.label: Stop the Steal / MAGA Movement Grassroots
data.motivation: Grassroots supporters who genuinely believe the election was stolen. Many attended January 6 rallies and some breached the Capitol. Their belief is sincere even though it is unsupported by evidence. Many have suffered legal consequences (1,200+ charged, 900+ convicted as of 2024). They represent the human cost of the fraud narrative: people who took action based on false information promoted by leaders who (in the case of Fox hosts and some campaign officials) privately knew the claims were false.

data.label: 4chan /pol/ anonymous users
data.motivation: Mix of ideological opposition to the Democratic Party (particularly Hillary Clinton's 2016 campaign), trolling culture where extreme claims are performatively escalated, and genuine conspiratorial belief. The timing (one week before the 2016 election) strongly suggests partisan motivation for initial framers, though many subsequent participants believed the claims sincerely.
data.name: actor:4chan-pol-anonymous-users

data.label: Alex Jones / InfoWars
data.motivation: Audience engagement and commercial revenue. Jones was among the most prominent amplifiers of Pizzagate, devoting multiple segments to the claims in November-December 2016. InfoWars generated estimated $50-80M annual peak revenue primarily through supplement sales. Pizzagate content drove significant traffic. Jones later issued a rare partial retraction regarding Comet Ping Pong (March 2017) after legal threats from Alefantis, though he continued promoting adjacent trafficking conspiracy narratives.
data.name: actor:alex-jones-infowars

data.label: Mike Cernovich
data.motivation: Audience building and political influence. Cernovich positioned himself as an alternative media figure during the 2016 election cycle. Amplifying Pizzagate claims built his audience and credibility within right-wing media. He later pivoted to broader 'elite pedophilia' framing, filing legal motions to unseal Epstein-related documents (2019), which allowed him to claim vindication when Epstein was arrested.
data.name: actor:mike-cernovich

data.label: Jack Posobiec
data.motivation: Political influence and media career advancement. Posobiec live-streamed from inside Comet Ping Pong in November 2016, presenting it as 'investigative journalism.' He was later identified as having connections to the US Navy intelligence community and right-wing political operations. Now a commentator on Turning Point USA and other conservative platforms.
data.name: actor:jack-posobiec

data.label: QAnon (the 'Q' persona and community)
data.motivation: The original 'Q' poster (first appearing on 4chan on October 28, 2017) adopted and expanded Pizzagate into a grand unified conspiracy theory involving a 'satanic cabal' of elites. Motivation of original Q persona is unknown. The QAnon community is sustained by a mix of sincere believers, grifters selling merchandise and subscriptions, political operatives, and social media algorithms. Ron Watkins, administrator of 8kun (formerly 8chan), has been identified by multiple investigations (HBO documentary 'Q: Into the Storm,' 2021) as a likely Q poster, though he denies it.
data.name: actor:qanon-the-q-persona-and-community

data.label: Edgar Maddison Welch
data.motivation: Sincere belief. Welch drove from North Carolina to Washington D.C. on December 4, 2016, entered Comet Ping Pong armed with an AR-15, a .38 revolver, and a folding knife, and fired the rifle inside the restaurant while searching for evidence of child trafficking. He found none. He told police he was 'self-investigating' the Pizzagate claims. He was sentenced to 4 years in prison (June 2017). Welch represents the archetypal 'cultivated ignorant agent': a person with genuine concern for children who was radicalized by fabricated claims into committing a violent act.
data.name: actor:edgar-maddison-welch

data.label: WikiLeaks
data.motivation: WikiLeaks published the Podesta emails beginning October 7, 2016, as part of its broader publication of materials related to the 2016 US election. The timing (one month before the election, following the Access Hollywood tape release) and selective release pattern have been characterized by US intelligence agencies as coordinated with Russian intelligence operations. WikiLeaks itself did not promote the Pizzagate interpretation, but the email releases provided the raw material.
data.name: actor:wikileaks

data.label: Russian intelligence operations (GRU / Internet Research Agency)
data.motivation: Strategic interference in the 2016 US election and broader erosion of trust in US institutions. The Internet Research Agency (IRA, based in St. Petersburg) amplified Pizzagate-related content through fake social media accounts. The GRU obtained and provided the Podesta emails to WikiLeaks. The Russian strategic interest is in sowing division and undermining democratic institutions, not in the specific Pizzagate claims.
data.name: actor:russian-intelligence-operations-gru-internet-research-agency

data.label: Legitimate anti-trafficking organizations (NCMEC, Polaris Project, Thorn)
data.motivation: Genuine child protection through evidence-based programs. These organizations have been forced to repeatedly clarify that QAnon and Pizzagate narratives do not reflect how trafficking actually works and have actively harmed anti-trafficking efforts by overwhelming hotlines with false tips and diverting public attention from evidence-based interventions.
data.name: actor:legitimate-anti-trafficking-organizations-ncmec-polaris-project-thorn

data.label: Mainstream investigative journalism (Miami Herald, New York Times, BBC)
data.motivation: Accountability journalism. Julie K. Brown's 'Perversion of Justice' series for the Miami Herald (2018) was instrumental in reopening the Epstein case. The NYT broke the Harvey Weinstein story (2017) that catalyzed #MeToo. The BBC's investigations into Jimmy Savile (2012) exposed decades of institutional protection. These investigations demonstrate that journalism, not conspiracy theory, has been the most effective mechanism for exposing real elite abuse.

data.label: QAnon merchandise and subscription economy
data.motivation: Direct commercial profit from QAnon and Pizzagate-adjacent beliefs. Includes sellers of QAnon merchandise (clothing, flags, stickers), subscription-based 'decode' channels, self-published books, and paid speaking events. The 'Patriot economy' around QAnon has been estimated in the hundreds of millions of dollars.

Facebook internal research (Frances Haugen disclosures, 2021) documented that the platform's algorithms preferentially surfaced conspiratorial and divisive content. YouTube's recommendation algorithm was documented directing users from mainstream news to conspiracy content.

data.label: GP-01
data.claim: A shadowy group of global elites (WEF, Bilderberg, etc.) secretly controls world governments and is planning a 'Great Reset' to establish a new world order.
data.name: scenario:GP-01

data.label: World Economic Forum (Klaus Schwab)
data.motivation: Promoting stakeholder capitalism, facilitating public-private cooperation, and enhancing the WEF's relevance as a convening platform. The WEF is funded by corporate membership fees ($60,000-$600,000 annually) and generates revenue from events, publications, and initiatives. Schwab has been the forum's chairman since its founding in 1971. The WEF's motivation is institutional self-perpetuation and influence through convening power, not secret global governance.
data.name: actor:world-economic-forum-klaus-schwab

data.label: Bilderberg Group
data.motivation: Facilitating informal, off-the-record discussions between North American and European political, business, and intellectual leaders. Established in 1954 during the Cold War to strengthen transatlantic relations. The group has no formal structure, no headquarters, no staff beyond a small secretariat, no budget for policy implementation, and no ability to compel any action from participants.
data.name: actor:bilderberg-group

data.label: QAnon Movement
data.motivation: QAnon (originating from anonymous posts on 4chan/8chan in October 2017) integrated NWO conspiracy framing into a broader narrative involving a 'deep state' cabal, child trafficking, and a coming 'Great Awakening.' The movement has no formal leadership or organization. It absorbed and amplified pre-existing conspiracy frameworks (Illuminati, NWO, Bilderberg) while adding new elements (Trump as savior figure, military tribunals, QAnon 'drops').
data.name: actor:qanon-movement

data.label: Tucker Carlson (formerly Fox News)
data.motivation: Audience engagement and political influence. Carlson amplified Great Reset and WEF conspiracy content to his audience of 3+ million nightly viewers (peak), framing WEF initiatives as evidence of elite plans to control ordinary citizens. His coverage introduced NWO-adjacent framing to mainstream conservative audiences who might not have encountered it through fringe channels.
data.name: actor:tucker-carlson-formerly-fox-news

data.label: Anti-Globalization Academic Left (Chomsky, Klein, etc.)
data.motivation: Structural critique of neoliberal globalization, corporate influence on democracy, and international economic institutions. Naomi Klein's 'The Shock Doctrine' (2007) documented how crises are exploited for neoliberal policy changes. Chomsky's work on manufacturing consent analyzed corporate-state power relationships. These critiques are empirically grounded, policy-specific, and advocate for democratic reform rather than conspiracy theories about secret control.
data.name: actor:anti-globalization-academic-left-chomsky-klein-etc

data.label: Right-Wing Populist Politicians (Trump, Bolsonaro, AfD, etc.)
data.motivation: Electoral mobilization through anti-elite framing. Politicians including Donald Trump, Jair Bolsonaro, AfD leadership, and various European populist parties use NWO-adjacent rhetoric ('globalists,' 'deep state,' 'establishment elites') to position themselves as champions of ordinary people against corrupt elites. This framing is used for electoral gain while the same politicians often pursue policies that benefit economic elites (tax cuts, deregulation).
data.name: actor:right-wing-populist-politicians-trump-bolsonaro-afd-etc

data.label: Elon Musk / X (formerly Twitter)
data.motivation: Musk has amplified WEF and Great Reset conspiracy content on X/Twitter since his acquisition (October 2022). His promotion of anti-WEF content reaches his 100M+ followers and shapes the platform's algorithmic amplification. Musk has personally tweeted WEF conspiracy framing while himself being a billionaire who attends elite forums and has received substantial government contracts and subsidies.
data.name: actor:elon-musk-x-formerly-twitter

data.label: Russian State Media (RT, Sputnik) and Influence Operations
data.motivation: Strategic amplification of narratives that erode trust in Western democratic institutions. NWO, WEF, and Great Reset conspiracy content serves Russia's geopolitical interest in weakening Western cohesion. RT has published extensive content promoting the idea that Western institutions are secretly controlled by a cabal of elites.
data.name: actor:russian-state-media-rt-sputnik-and-influence-operations

data.label: Gilens & Page / Political Science Research
data.motivation: Academic research on democratic representation and elite influence. Gilens and Page's 2014 study, based on analysis of 1,779 policy issues, found that economic elites and organized business groups had substantially more influence on US policy outcomes than average citizens. This is empirical political science, not conspiracy theory. The study's policy implications support democratic reform (campaign finance reform, lobbying restrictions, voting access), not withdrawal from democratic participation.
data.name: actor:gilens-page-political-science-research

data.label: Anti-WEF/Anti-Lockdown Movements (Freedom Convoy, Querdenker, etc.)
data.motivation: These movements emerged during the COVID-19 pandemic as grassroots responses to government restrictions (lockdowns, vaccine mandates, vaccine passports). The Canadian Freedom Convoy (January-February 2022), Germany's Querdenker movement, and similar groups across Europe combined legitimate concerns about government overreach with conspiracy framing about the Great Reset. They provided physical, real-world mobilization around narratives that had previously circulated primarily online.
data.name: actor:anti-wef-anti-lockdown-movements-freedom-convoy-querdenker-etc

data.label: GP-02
data.claim: A secret New World Order of elites controls all world governments from behind the scenes.
data.name: scenario:GP-02

data.label: John Birch Society
data.motivation: Founded in 1958 by Robert Welch Jr. as an anti-communist organization. Initially focused on opposing communism, it evolved to promote a conspiratorial worldview in which both communism and liberal internationalism were orchestrated by a secret elite. The NWO narrative served to explain why the US government pursued policies the JBS opposed (UN membership, foreign aid, civil rights legislation). Revenue from membership dues, publications, and speaking tours.
data.name: actor:john-birch-society

data.label: Gary Allen
data.motivation: Author and JBS-affiliated journalist whose 'None Dare Call It Conspiracy' (1971) became the most influential popular text of the NWO conspiracy tradition. Motivated by anti-communist and anti-internationalist ideology. The book was distributed as campaign literature during the 1972 presidential election.
data.name: actor:gary-allen

data.label: Pat Robertson
data.motivation: Televangelist and founder of the Christian Broadcasting Network (CBN) and the Christian Coalition. His 1991 book 'The New World Order' merged NWO conspiracy theory with evangelical eschatology, framing the conspiracy as a satanic plot fulfilling biblical prophecy. This fusion dramatically expanded the audience for NWO claims by connecting them to pre-existing end-times beliefs held by millions of evangelical Christians. Revenue from book sales, CBN donations, and political influence.
data.name: actor:pat-robertson

data.label: David Icke
data.motivation: Former BBC sports presenter turned conspiracy author. Since 'The Biggest Secret' (1999), Icke has promoted a variation of NWO theory involving 'Reptilian' shapeshifting extraterrestrials controlling world governments. Revenue from book sales (over 20 books), speaking tours (sold-out events at Wembley Arena), and website subscriptions (davidicke.com). His addition of the reptilian layer both expanded and insulated the narrative: any debunking could be dismissed as not addressing the 'real' (extraterrestrial) controllers.
data.name: actor:david-icke

data.label: Daniel Estulin
data.motivation: Lithuanian-born author who claims to have infiltrated Bilderberg Group security. His book 'The True Story of the Bilderberg Group' (2005, English translation 2009) became the primary popular text alleging Bilderberg as the operational center of NWO governance. Revenue from book sales (translated into 60+ languages according to publisher claims) and speaking engagements.
data.name: actor:daniel-estulin

data.label: Council on Foreign Relations (CFR)
data.motivation: Independent think tank founded in 1921 to promote informed public debate about US foreign policy. Publishes Foreign Affairs journal, one of the most influential international relations publications. Membership includes former presidents, secretaries of state, CEOs, and academics. The CFR's real influence on policy debate is well-documented; the conspiracy claim is that it controls policy rather than influences discussion.
data.name: actor:council-on-foreign-relations-cfr

data.label: Academic researchers on elite power (Mills, Domhoff, Gilens, Page, Winters)
data.motivation: Legitimate scholarly analysis of how wealth and institutional position translate into political influence. C. Wright Mills ('The Power Elite', 1956), G. William Domhoff ('Who Rules America?', 1967), Gilens and Page (2014), and Jeffrey Winters ('Oligarchy', 2011) provide empirical documentation of elite influence. Their work is frequently misquoted by conspiracy theorists to support claims of deliberate, unified control.
data.name: actor:academic-researchers-on-elite-power-mills-domhoff-gilens-page-winters

data.label: Russian state media (RT, Sputnik) and state-aligned influence operations
data.motivation: Strategic amplification of NWO narratives to undermine trust in Western democratic institutions and NATO cohesion. The EU East StratCom Task Force's EUvsDisinfo database documents systematic RT and Sputnik amplification of NWO and 'Great Reset' conspiracy content. Russian information warfare doctrine (as described in the Gerasimov Doctrine, 2013) explicitly targets societal cohesion.
data.name: actor:russian-state-media-rt-sputnik-and-state-aligned-influence-operations

data.label: Social media platforms and algorithm-driven content recommendation
data.motivation: Engagement-maximizing algorithms that preferentially surface sensational, conspiratorial content because it generates higher engagement metrics (watch time, shares, comments). YouTube's recommendation algorithm has been documented directing users from mainstream political content to conspiracy material (Guillaume Chaslot, AlgoTransparency, 2018). Platform financial incentive is advertising revenue.

data.label: Scapegoating
data.how_it_works: Scapegoating works by taking a real problem with multiple causes and attributing it entirely to a single, emotionally salient outgroup. The technique has three components: (1) Identify a genuine grievance the audience feels (NHS waiting times, housing costs, school pressures). (2) Present a visible, easily identified outgroup as the cause (immigrants). (3) Suppress or dismiss the actual primary causes (austerity, underinvestment, planning failures, policy choices). The power of scapegoating is that the grievance IS real, so the audience's emotional response is genuine. The manipulation is in the attribution, not the emotion. Once the attribution is established, the scapegoat becomes a container for diffuse anxieties that are otherwise difficult to direct: economic insecurity, cultural change, loss of status, political powerlessness all get channeled toward a single target. This makes the technique self-reinforcing: as long as the underlying anxieties persist (and they will, because the scapegoated group is not the actual cause), the demand for scapegoating intensifies.
data.historical_precedent: Scapegoating is one of the oldest manipulation techniques in political history. The Dreyfus Affair (France, 1894) scapegoated a Jewish officer for systemic military failures. McCarthyism (US, 1950s) scapegoated 'communists' for complex geopolitical anxieties. Enoch Powell's 'Rivers of Blood' speech (1968) used identical causal attribution (immigrants cause social decline) with identical emotional escalation in the UK specifically. The tobacco industry scapegoated individual smoker choice to deflect from corporate responsibility for addiction. In each case, the pattern is the same: real anxieties, wrong attribution, suppression of actual causes.
data.name: reveal:scapegoating

data.label: Emotional Amplification
data.how_it_works: Emotional amplification takes a measured factual statement and escalates its emotional register until the audience responds to the emotion rather than evaluating the evidence. The technique works at the language level: 'immigration is increasing' becomes 'we're being invaded.' 'Some asylum seekers arrive by boat' becomes 'an armada is crossing the Channel.' 'Public services face pressure' becomes 'our country is being destroyed.' Each escalation step makes the statement less accurate but more emotionally compelling and more shareable. The amplified version displaces the measured version in public discourse because emotional content outcompetes factual content for attention, memory, and sharing behaviour. Crucially, emotional amplification makes counter-evidence feel like a personal attack: if you FEEL invaded, being told the numbers are modest feels like a dismissal of your experience.
data.historical_precedent: Joseph Goebbels' propaganda principles explicitly codified emotional amplification: 'propaganda must appeal to the emotions rather than the intellect.' The US War on Terror escalated 'terrorist threat' into existential civilizational conflict. Anti-vaccine movements amplify 'vaccine adverse events exist' into 'vaccines are killing children.' The Fox News model pioneered emotional escalation of news content as a business strategy. In each case, the amplification serves to overwhelm rational evaluation with emotional response.
data.name: reveal:emotional-amplification

data.label: False Dichotomy
data.how_it_works: False dichotomy presents a complex issue as having only two possible positions, eliminating the middle ground where most people actually live. The technique works by collapsing a spectrum into a binary: you are either FOR restricting immigration or you are AGAINST the British people. You either support Farage's approach or you support 'open borders.' You either acknowledge the problem or you are part of the elite that caused it. The false dichotomy eliminates the position held by most experts and most of the public: that immigration is a complex policy issue requiring evidence-based management that balances economic needs, humanitarian obligations, and community impact. By eliminating this middle ground, the technique forces people to 'pick a side' and makes nuanced positions feel like evasion.
data.historical_precedent: George W. Bush's 'you're either with us or against us' (post-9/11) is the textbook modern false dichotomy. Brexit itself was a false dichotomy: a binary referendum on a spectrum of possible UK-EU relationships. The US abortion debate is framed as 'pro-life vs. pro-choice' when most Americans hold intermediate positions. False dichotomy is a staple of populist movements because it forces allegiance and punishes nuance.
data.name: reveal:false-dichotomy

data.label: Cherry-Picking Statistics
data.how_it_works: Cherry-picking works by selecting the most alarming data point from a complex evidence base and presenting it without context. The technique has three steps: (1) Find the metric that supports your narrative (e.g., gross immigration numbers rather than net contribution). (2) Present it in the most alarming frame (absolute numbers rather than per-capita, short time periods that show spikes rather than long-term trends). (3) Omit the context that would change the interpretation (fiscal contribution, labour market needs, demographic dependency ratios). The power of cherry-picking is that each individual statistic is technically accurate, so fact-checkers cannot say 'that number is wrong.' They can only say 'that number is misleading without context,' which is a much harder message to communicate.
data.historical_precedent: The tobacco industry cherry-picked studies showing no cancer link while suppressing its own internal research showing the opposite. Climate denial cherry-picks cold weather events to dispute long-term warming trends. Anti-vaccine advocates cherry-pick VAERS reports without denominators. The technique is ubiquitous in motivated reasoning and particularly effective when the full evidence base is complex enough that most people cannot evaluate it independently.
data.name: reveal:cherry-picking-statistics

data.label: Sanewashing / Normalization Through Coverage
data.how_it_works: Sanewashing works by translating extreme positions into reasonable-sounding language through the editorial process. A politician says 'we are being invaded by illegal immigrants'; the news presenter says 'Mr Farage expressed concern about rising immigration numbers.' A politician uses dehumanizing rhetoric; the coverage summarizes it as 'strong language.' A politician makes a factually false claim; the coverage presents it as 'his view' rather than evaluating it against evidence. The technique is usually not deliberate: it emerges from journalistic norms around 'balance,' 'fairness,' and 'letting the audience decide.' But its effect is to launder extreme rhetoric through the credibility of mainstream media institutions. Once the BBC has presented a position as part of legitimate debate, it becomes much harder to argue that the position is outside the bounds of evidence-based discourse.
data.historical_precedent: CNN's coverage of Trump in 2015-2016 provided billions of dollars in free media coverage and normalized positions that were previously considered fringe. The US media's 'both sides' coverage of climate change (giving equal weight to scientific consensus and industry-funded denial) delayed public understanding of climate science by decades. The BBC's own coverage of the MMR-autism hoax gave 'balance' to Andrew Wakefield's fraudulent claims alongside the medical consensus. In each case, the journalistic norm of 'balance' produced a distortion that favoured the less evidence-supported position.
data.name: reveal:sanewashing-normalization-through-coverage

data.label: Emotional Amplification (by the claim-maker)
data.how_it_works: The claim-maker uses the same emotional amplification technique they are criticizing, albeit at a much smaller scale. 'Lies being recycled' is emotionally charged where 'debunked claims being repeated' would be more precise. 'Too ashamed to admit' attributes a psychological state to an entire nation, which is an overgeneralization. 'Built on the same lies' frames Brexit in maximally negative terms where 'based substantially on misleading claims' would be more accurate.
data.historical_precedent: This is common in political criticism: critics of manipulation often adopt emotional framing to compete for attention. Anti-Trump commentary frequently uses amplified language. Anti-Brexit commentary uses 'catastrophe' and 'disaster' where economic data shows 'significant but not catastrophic impact.' The dynamic is that measured criticism gets less attention than emotional criticism, creating an incentive for critics to match the emotional register of the content they oppose.
data.name: reveal:emotional-amplification-by-the-claim-maker

data.motivation: Systematic debunking of chemtrail and other conspiracy claims. West, a retired video game developer (co-created Tony Hawk's Pro Skater), has methodically addressed chemtrail evidence through detailed analysis of contrail physics, aircraft identification via FlightRadar24, and atmospheric science.
data.name: actor:metabunk-org-mick-west

data.label: Cherry Picking
data.how_it_works: Selecting only the evidence that supports a predetermined conclusion while ignoring the vastly larger body of evidence that contradicts it. The technique works because the selected evidence is often real, making the argument feel grounded even as it systematically excludes context. The audience sees genuine data points and reasonably concludes 'the evidence supports this,' never realizing they are seeing a carefully curated subset. It is like a card trick where the magician shows you five cards from a deck of fifty-two and asks you to judge the whole deck by those five.
data.historical_precedent: The tobacco industry employed systematic cherry picking for decades, funding studies designed to find ambiguous results and then promoting only those results while suppressing the overwhelming evidence linking smoking to cancer. The Tobacco Industry Research Committee (1954-1998) spent billions selecting and amplifying doubt-producing research. The playbook was later adopted by fossil fuel companies questioning climate science (e.g., Exxon's internal research vs. public communications, documented by Supran and Oreskes, 2017, Environmental Research Letters).
data.name: reveal:cherry-picking

data.label: Appeal to Fear
data.how_it_works: Bypassing rational evaluation by activating deep-seated anxieties about physical safety, bodily autonomy, and helplessness against powerful institutions. The technique works because fear narrows attention, increases credulity toward threat-confirming information, and decreases receptiveness to reassurance. Evolutionary psychology explains why: our ancestors who responded strongly to potential threats survived more often than those who calmly evaluated evidence before fleeing a predator. This ancient wiring is maladaptive when applied to complex modern claims. Fear also creates urgency that drives sharing behavior, which is why fear-based content spreads faster than nuanced analysis on social media (Vosoughi, Roy, and Aral, 2018, Science).
data.historical_precedent: The 'Reefer Madness' anti-marijuana campaign (1936-1970s) used fear of insanity, violence, and moral corruption to drive public support for prohibition, with claims that bore little relationship to actual pharmacological effects. The campaign was eventually discredited but not before shaping decades of drug policy. Similarly, the Satanic Panic of the 1980s-1990s used fear for children's safety to generate false accusations of ritual abuse, with devastating consequences for falsely accused individuals. In both cases, genuine concern for safety was exploited to drive policy and behavior changes that the evidence did not support.
data.name: reveal:appeal-to-fear

data.label: Unfalsifiable Framing
data.how_it_works: Constructing claims in a way that makes them impossible to disprove, so that any evidence against the claim is reinterpreted as evidence for it. The technique works by inoculating believers against counter-evidence before they encounter it: 'they will deny it, which proves they are covering it up.' This creates a closed epistemic loop where the claim cannot be tested, because the test itself is defined as part of the conspiracy. It is the intellectual equivalent of a rigged game where heads-I-win, tails-you-lose. The power of the technique lies in its appeal to a sophisticated form of skepticism: 'of course the authorities deny it; what else would they do if it were true?' This reasoning pattern mimics critical thinking while actually shutting it down.
data.historical_precedent: Unfalsifiable framing is a hallmark of conspiracy theories across history. The 'Protocols of the Elders of Zion' (fabricated 1903, debunked repeatedly) used the same structure: any attempt to disprove the document was claimed to be part of the very conspiracy it described. Flat Earth theory employs identical logic: photos from space are faked, scientists are in on it, anyone who disagrees is deceived or compromised. The structure persists because it provides psychological certainty in an uncertain world, which is itself a powerful emotional reward.
data.name: reveal:unfalsifiable-framing

data.label: Credential Hijacking
data.how_it_works: Appropriating the language, methods, or institutional trappings of legitimate expertise to create an appearance of scientific authority without the underlying methodology. The technique works because most people (reasonably) use surface-level credibility signals to evaluate information: does this look like a research paper? Are there citations? Does the author use technical terminology? These heuristics work well for mainstream information but are exploitable by content that mimics the form of expertise while lacking its substance. The audience sees a document that looks like science and applies the trust they would give to actual science.
data.historical_precedent: Andrew Wakefield's fraudulent 1998 Lancet paper linking MMR vaccine to autism used the credential of peer-reviewed publication to give false credibility to fabricated data. Although retracted in 2010 and Wakefield struck off the medical register, the paper's authority-mimicking format allowed its claims to persist for over a decade before institutional accountability caught up. The tobacco industry funded the creation of seemingly independent research organizations (Council for Tobacco Research, 1954) specifically to produce credential-bearing doubt about the smoking-cancer link.
data.name: reveal:credential-hijacking

data.label: Andrew Wakefield
data.motivation: Originally a gastroenterologist at the Royal Free Hospital, London. Published the fraudulent 1998 Lancet paper that launched the vaccine-autism narrative. Had undisclosed financial conflicts: received over 435,000 GBP from lawyer Richard Barr to build a case against MMR manufacturers, and held a patent application (filed June 1997) for a competing single-dose measles vaccine that would only have commercial value if the combined MMR was withdrawn. After being struck from the UK medical register in 2010, relocated to Austin, Texas and reinvented himself as a documentary filmmaker and anti-vaccine movement leader. Directed 'Vaxxed' (2016) and 'Vaxxed II' (2019). Continues to profit from speaking engagements and movement leadership.
data.name: actor:andrew-wakefield

data.label: Robert F. Kennedy Jr. / Children's Health Defense
data.motivation: Environmental lawyer who pivoted to anti-vaccine advocacy. Founded the World Mercury Project (2016, later renamed Children's Health Defense). Published 'Deadly Immunity' in Rolling Stone and Salon (2005, retracted by both). Published 'The Real Anthony Fauci' (2021), which became a bestseller. Ran for president in 2024. Nominated as HHS Secretary in January 2025. CHD's revenue grew from $1.2M (2017) to $15.8M (2021), largely from donations from individuals convinced by the vaccine-autism narrative. Kennedy's anti-vaccine work provides him political identity, media attention, book sales, and ultimately political power.
data.name: actor:robert-f-kennedy-jr-children-s-health-defense

data.label: Jenny McCarthy
data.motivation: Celebrity whose son was diagnosed with autism. Became a prominent anti-vaccine advocate after attributing his condition to the MMR vaccine. Her 2007 Oprah appearance and subsequent books reached mass audiences. Became president of Generation Rescue (2008). Her emotional, personal narrative was more persuasive to general audiences than scientific studies. Later moderated her public position, claiming she was 'not anti-vaccine' but 'pro-safe-vaccine,' though the practical effect of her advocacy was vaccine avoidance.
data.name: actor:jenny-mccarthy

data.label: Del Bigtree / Informed Consent Action Network (ICAN)
data.motivation: Former television producer (The Doctors) who co-produced the Vaxxed documentary with Wakefield. Founded ICAN and hosts The HighWire, a weekly online show focused on vaccine skepticism and medical freedom. Audience grew significantly during COVID-19. ICAN has filed FOIA lawsuits against the CDC and HHS, framing routine document production as revelations. His media production skills make anti-vaccine content more professionally produced and emotionally compelling than typical science communication.
data.name: actor:del-bigtree-informed-consent-action-network-ican

data.label: Brian Hooker
data.motivation: Biochemical engineer and father of a child with autism. Recorded phone calls with William Thompson without consent and published a re-analysis of the DeStefano 2004 study that was retracted for undisclosed conflicts of interest and inappropriate statistical methods (using a case-control study as a cohort study). His personal motivation as a parent of an affected child is genuine, but his scientific methodology has been consistently rejected by the research community.
data.name: actor:brian-hooker

data.label: National Vaccine Information Center (NVIC)
data.motivation: Founded in 1982 by Barbara Loe Fisher following the DPT vaccine controversy. The oldest anti-vaccine advocacy organization in the United States. Frames itself as promoting 'informed consent' while systematically emphasizing vaccine risks and minimizing vaccine benefits. Has lobbied against vaccine mandates in state legislatures. Revenue from donations and merchandise. NVIC pre-dates the Wakefield paper and adopted the autism-vaccine narrative as it emerged.
data.name: actor:national-vaccine-information-center-nvic

data.label: Children's Health Defense (CHD)
data.motivation: Founded by RFK Jr. as the World Mercury Project (2016), renamed Children's Health Defense (2018). Revenue grew from $1.2M (2017) to $15.8M (2021). Publishes The Defender website, produces documentaries, funds litigation against vaccine mandates, and operates a large social media presence. Identified as the single largest organizational source of anti-vaccine misinformation by the Center for Countering Digital Hate.
data.name: actor:children-s-health-defense-chd

data.label: Joseph Mercola
data.motivation: Osteopathic physician and alternative medicine entrepreneur. His website Mercola.com has been one of the largest sources of anti-vaccine and alternative health content. Reportedly donated $2.9M to NVIC between 2012 and 2019. Sells dietary supplements and alternative health products. The FDA has repeatedly warned him about illegal health claims. His business model depends on undermining trust in conventional medicine to sell alternatives.
data.name: actor:joseph-mercola

data.label: Pharmaceutical companies (Merck, GSK, Sanofi Pasteur)
data.motivation: Manufacturers of childhood vaccines including MMR (Merck's M-M-R II). Commercial entities with profit motives. Vaccine revenue: Merck's vaccine division generated approximately $11.2B in 2023. The 1986 National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act provided liability protection for manufacturers, which was a legitimate policy compromise but is used by anti-vaccine advocates as evidence of dangerous products. Pharmaceutical companies have genuine conflicts of interest (profit from vaccine sales) but also face regulatory oversight from FDA, CDC ACIP, and independent review bodies.
data.name: actor:pharmaceutical-companies-merck-gsk-sanofi-pasteur

data.label: CDC / FDA / HHS (institutional science)
data.motivation: Federal agencies responsible for vaccine safety monitoring, approval, and recommendations. The CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) sets the childhood vaccine schedule. These agencies conduct and fund vaccine safety research. They have institutional interests in maintaining public confidence in the vaccine program. The revolving door concern (e.g., former CDC Director Julie Gerberding becoming Merck Vaccines president in 2009) is factual and raises legitimate conflict-of-interest questions, though it does not constitute evidence of data fraud.
data.name: actor:cdc-fda-hhs-institutional-science

data.label: Independent research community (global)
data.motivation: Researchers in epidemiology, immunology, genetics, and developmental neuroscience across universities and research institutions worldwide. Studies disproving the vaccine-autism link come from the UK, Denmark, Japan, Finland, Canada, and the United States, conducted by independent teams with no common funding source or coordination. A global conspiracy to suppress evidence would require coordinating thousands of independent researchers across dozens of countries with competing national interests.
data.name: actor:independent-research-community-global

data.label: Social media platforms
data.motivation: Facebook, YouTube, Twitter/X, TikTok, and Instagram serve as primary distribution channels for anti-vaccine content. Engagement-driven algorithms amplify emotional and controversial content. Anti-vaccine content generates high engagement through fear, outrage, and identity signaling. Platforms have implemented varying degrees of content moderation: Facebook added information labels (2019), YouTube reduced recommendations of anti-vaccine content (2019), but enforcement has been inconsistent. Twitter/X under Musk reduced content moderation beginning in 2022.

Thompson's own formal statement (released through his attorney Morgan Verkamp) said he believed vaccines did not cause autism and that all children should be vaccinated. He expressed concerns about a specific subgroup analysis decision, not about a cover-up of a definitive link. His statements were taken out of context and used against his stated position.

data.label: HS-03
data.claim: 5G cell towers cause cancer and were responsible for spreading COVID-19.
data.name: scenario:HS-03

data.label: Thomas Cowan, MD (license revoked)
data.motivation: Anthroposophic physician influenced by Rudolf Steiner's spiritual science framework. Cowan's worldview attributes disease to toxicity and electromagnetic disruption rather than germ theory. His March 2020 presentation claiming viruses are cellular responses to EMF went viral. He appears motivated by genuine conviction in an alternative medical paradigm.
data.name: actor:thomas-cowan-md-license-revoked

data.label: Mark Steele
data.motivation: Self-described weapons systems expert from Gateshead, UK who became one of the earliest and most vocal 5G opposition campaigners. Claims 5G streetlights are 'directed energy weapons.' He has filed multiple legal challenges against 5G installations. Motivated by apparent genuine conviction and local activist following.
data.name: actor:mark-steele

data.label: Keri Hilson (musician)
data.motivation: Not a deliberate disinformation actor. A musician with 2.4 million Twitter followers who shared 5G-COVID correlation content in March 2020, likely as a genuinely concerned citizen. Her viral tweet (March 16, 2020) reached millions before deletion. Example of how celebrity reach can amplify conspiracy narratives through sincere but uninformed sharing.
data.name: actor:keri-hilson-musician

data.label: RT America / Sputnik (Russian state media)
data.motivation: Strategic amplification of narratives that undermine trust in Western institutions and infrastructure. RT America produced a documentary segment 'A Dangerous Game?' in 2019 highlighting 5G health fears before the COVID pandemic. This pre-positioned amplification of 5G anxiety was noted by the European Commission's EUvsDisinfo project. RT's coverage provides 'legitimate media' veneer to fringe claims.
data.name: actor:rt-america-sputnik-russian-state-media

data.label: Stop 5G movement (grassroots)
data.motivation: Loosely coordinated global network of local activist groups opposing 5G infrastructure deployment. Includes groups like Stop 5G UK, Australians for Safe Technology, and Americans for Responsible Technology. Motivated by a mix of genuine health concern, property value anxiety, and opposition to increased electromagnetic exposure. Most participants are sincere citizens, not deliberate disinformation actors.
data.name: actor:stop-5g-movement-grassroots

data.label: ICNIRP (International Commission on Non-Ionizing Radiation Protection)
data.motivation: Independent scientific body established in 1992 that sets international EMF exposure guidelines. Reviewed the evidence and updated its RF-EMF guidelines in March 2020, maintaining that 5G within guideline limits does not pose a health risk. Operates under formal charter with WHO collaboration.
data.name: actor:icnirp-international-commission-on-non-ionizing-radiation-protection

data.label: WHO / IARC
data.motivation: The World Health Organization and its International Agency for Research on Cancer provide independent health risk assessments. IARC classified RF-EMF as Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic) in 2011. WHO has repeatedly stated there is no evidence 5G poses a health risk within current exposure guidelines. A re-evaluation of RF-EMF carcinogenicity is pending on the IARC Monographs priority list.
data.name: actor:who-iarc

data.label: Telecommunications industry (GSMA, major carriers)
data.motivation: Multi-billion dollar financial interest in 5G deployment. Global 5G infrastructure investment projected at $1.7 trillion through 2025 (IHS Markit). Carriers have actively funded safety research and lobbied against stricter EMF regulation. The industry's financial stake in 5G deployment creates a legitimate conflict of interest that conspiracy narratives exploit.
data.name: actor:telecommunications-industry-gsma-major-carriers

data.label: National Toxicology Program (NTP)
data.motivation: US government interagency program that conducted the largest and most expensive ($30M) study of RF-EMF and cancer in rats, reporting results in 2018. The NTP is a credible research body; however, its findings are complex and have been selectively cited by both sides of the debate.
data.name: actor:national-toxicology-program-ntp

data.label: Full Fact / Reuters Fact Check / AFP Fact Check
data.motivation: Independent fact-checking organizations that debunked 5G-COVID claims during the pandemic. Full Fact (UK) was particularly active given the UK cell tower arson attacks. Reuters and AFP published multiple fact-check articles in spring 2020.
data.name: actor:full-fact-reuters-fact-check-afp-fact-check

data.label: EMF-blocking product industry
data.motivation: Direct commercial profit from EMF and 5G fears. Companies sell anti-5G products including shielding phone cases ($25-60), 'EMF harmonizers' ($40-200), faraday cages ($50-500), EMF-blocking paint ($100-300/liter), and '5G protection' stickers and pendants ($15-80). Some products have been tested and found to have no measurable shielding effect.
data.name: actor:emf-blocking-product-industry

data.label: HS-04
data.claim: Big Pharma is suppressing natural cures for cancer to protect their profits.
data.name: scenario:HS-04

data.label: Mike Adams / NaturalNews
data.motivation: Revenue through advertising, supplement sales, and site traffic. NaturalNews is one of the most-visited alternative health websites, generating millions of monthly page views. Adams promotes claims that pharmaceutical companies suppress natural cures while selling alternative health products. The FDA identified NaturalNews as a top source of COVID-19 health misinformation (2020). Adams also operates supplement brands and prepper product lines.
data.name: actor:mike-adams-naturalnews

data.label: Ty and Charlene Bollinger / The Truth About Cancer
data.motivation: Revenue from documentary sales, health product affiliates, and supplement promotion. The 'Truth About Cancer' docuseries (launched 2014) presents the narrative that pharmaceutical companies and regulatory agencies suppress effective natural cancer treatments. The franchise expanded to include 'The Truth About Vaccines' and generates revenue through product sales and affiliate marketing.
data.name: actor:ty-and-charlene-bollinger-the-truth-about-cancer

data.label: Gwyneth Paltrow / Goop
data.motivation: Commercial revenue from lifestyle and health product sales. Goop (founded 2008, valued at $250M+ by 2020) has promoted unproven health products including vaginal jade eggs, 'psychic vampire repellent,' and various supplements with unsubstantiated health claims. While not specifically focused on cancer cure suppression, Goop contributes to the broader ecosystem of distrust toward conventional medicine and promotion of unproven alternatives.
data.name: actor:gwyneth-paltrow-goop

data.label: US Supplement Industry (NPA, CRN)
data.motivation: Commercial: the US dietary supplement market exceeds $50 billion annually (Grand View Research, 2023). Industry trade organizations including the Natural Products Association (NPA) and Council for Responsible Nutrition (CRN) lobby to maintain the permissive regulatory framework established by DSHEA (1994). The industry benefits from consumer distrust of pharmaceutical medicine, which drives demand for alternative products.
data.name: actor:us-supplement-industry-npa-crn

data.label: Pharmaceutical Industry (PhRMA member companies)
data.motivation: Commercial: global pharmaceutical revenue was approximately $1.48 trillion in 2022 (IQVIA). Companies invest in drug development (average $2.6 billion per approved drug, DiMasi et al., 2016) to generate returns through patent-protected sales periods. The industry has documented cases of prioritizing profits over safety (Vioxx, OxyContin) and of pricing practices that harm patients (insulin pricing, Shkreli). These legitimate criticisms provide the fertile ground from which the unfounded suppression narrative grows.
data.name: actor:pharmaceutical-industry-phrma-member-companies

data.label: NCI / NIH / Academic Research Institutions
data.motivation: Research mission: the NCI's annual budget is approximately $7.3 billion (FY2024). The NCI Natural Products Branch has been screening natural compounds for anticancer activity since 1960. Academic researchers at universities worldwide study natural compounds without pharmaceutical industry funding. These institutions have no profit motive for suppressing effective treatments and would gain enormous prestige from discovering one.
data.name: actor:nci-nih-academic-research-institutions

data.label: FDA (US Food and Drug Administration)
data.motivation: Regulatory mission: ensuring safety and efficacy of drugs, devices, and food. The FDA's drug review budget is approximately 65% funded by industry user fees (PDUFA), creating a perceived conflict of interest. However, the FDA has approved numerous natural-product-derived drugs and has a specific framework for botanical drug development (Guidance for Industry: Botanical Drug Products, updated 2016).
data.name: actor:fda-us-food-and-drug-administration

data.label: Universal Healthcare Systems (NHS, German statutory insurance, Japanese NHI, etc.)
data.motivation: These systems are government-funded and have a direct financial incentive to minimize treatment costs. If effective, cheap natural cancer cures existed, these systems would have every incentive to adopt them. The NHS (UK), which spends approximately GBP 6.3 billion annually on cancer treatment (Cancer Research UK estimate), would save billions by switching to effective natural treatments. The fact that no universal healthcare system anywhere in the world has adopted 'natural cures' as standard cancer treatment is strong evidence against the suppression thesis.
data.name: actor:universal-healthcare-systems-nhs-german-statutory-insurance-japanese-nhi-etc

data.label: Social Media Platforms (YouTube, Facebook/Meta, Instagram, TikTok)
data.motivation: Engagement-driven algorithms that amplify emotionally charged health content. 'What your doctor does not want you to know' content generates high engagement. Alternative cancer cure videos, testimonials, and before/after stories perform well algorithmically. Platforms have implemented health misinformation policies with inconsistent enforcement.
data.name: actor:social-media-platforms-youtube-facebook-meta-instagram-tiktok

data.label: HS-05
data.claim: Water fluoridation is mass medication without informed consent.
data.name: scenario:HS-05

data.label: Fluoride Action Network (FAN) / Paul Connett
data.motivation: FAN is the primary organized opposition to water fluoridation globally, founded in 2000 by Paul Connett, a retired chemistry professor from St. Lawrence University. Connett is co-author of 'The Case Against Fluoride' (2010, Chelsea Green Publishing). FAN operates as a 501(c)(3) nonprofit. Its advocacy combines legitimate scientific criticism (citing Cochrane reviews, NTP monograph) with broader anti-fluoridation campaigning. FAN was the organizational force behind the TSCA lawsuit (Food & Water Watch v. EPA) that resulted in Judge Chen's 2024 ruling.
data.name: actor:fluoride-action-network-fan-paul-connett

data.label: US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
data.motivation: CDC has promoted water fluoridation since its inception and named community water fluoridation one of the '10 great public health achievements of the 20th century' in 1999. CDC's Division of Oral Health manages the Community Water Fluoridation program. The agency has a strong institutional investment in fluoridation as established public health policy.
data.name: actor:us-centers-for-disease-control-and-prevention-cdc

data.label: American Dental Association (ADA)
data.motivation: ADA has been the strongest institutional advocate for water fluoridation since endorsing it in 1950, making it one of the first national health organizations to do so. ADA's support is central to its professional identity around preventive dentistry. ADA provides toolkits for communities considering fluoridation and actively opposes ballot measures to remove fluoride.
data.name: actor:american-dental-association-ada

data.label: Judge Edward Chen / US District Court, Northern District of California
data.motivation: Federal judge presiding over Food & Water Watch v. EPA (TSCA lawsuit). Issued ruling in September 2024 after a bench trial with extensive expert testimony from both sides.
data.name: actor:judge-edward-chen-us-district-court-northern-district-of-california

data.label: Cochrane Collaboration
data.motivation: Independent organization that produces systematic reviews of healthcare interventions. Conducted a systematic review of water fluoridation in 2015 (Iheozor-Ejiofor et al.).
data.name: actor:cochrane-collaboration

data.label: World Health Organization (WHO)
data.motivation: WHO includes fluoridation as one of several recommended approaches to population-level caries prevention, alongside salt fluoridation and milk fluoridation. WHO's recommended optimal fluoride concentration is 0.5-1.0 mg/L.
data.name: actor:world-health-organization-who

data.label: International Association of Oral Medicine and Toxicology (IAOMT)
data.motivation: Professional organization that opposes both fluoridation and dental amalgam (mercury fillings). Positions itself as a science-based alternative voice within dentistry.
data.name: actor:international-association-of-oral-medicine-and-toxicology-iaomt

data.label: National Research Council (NRC) Committee on Fluoride in Drinking Water
data.motivation: NRC convened an expert committee at EPA's request to review the safety of fluoride in drinking water. Published their report in March 2006.
data.name: actor:national-research-council-nrc-committee-on-fluoride-in-drinking-water

data.label: Nuffield Council on Bioethics
data.motivation: Independent UK bioethics body that examined the ethics of public health interventions including fluoridation in their 2007 report 'Public Health: Ethical Issues.'
data.name: actor:nuffield-council-on-bioethics
